title: Surgical theater nonoperative period analysis and optimization
abstract: Various of the disclosed embodiments provide systems and methods for assessing surgical team performance during nonoperative periods within the surgical theater. Particularly, one or more sensors placed throughout the surgical theater may acquire data during the nonoperative period, and in some embodiments, over the course of several procedures and nonoperative periods within the theater. Disclosed embodiments segment and analyze this data to infer undesirable or inefficient behavior, as well as progress in improving behaviors, during the periods between surgical procedures. A plurality of topologies and metrics for assessing nonoperative periods are also disclosed, as well as systems and methods for monitoring and providing feedback.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS We claim: 1 . A computer-implemented method for assessing team member performance during a nonoperative period in a surgical theater, the method comprising: detecting a team member from theater-wide data acquired during the nonoperative period; determining a motion of the team member; and determining a first metric result based, at least in part, upon the motion of the team member. 2. The computer-implemented method of Claim 1 , wherein the theater-wide data comprises theater-wide visual image data and theater-wide depth data. 3. The computer-implemented method of Claim 2, wherein, the team member is associated with a role performed in the theater, and wherein, detecting the team member comprises determining that the team member is associated with the role. 4. The computer-implemented method of Claim 3, the method further comprising: detecting an interval in the theater-wide data; determining a duration of the interval; and determining a second metric result based, at least in part, upon the duration of the interval. 5. The computer-implemented method of Claim 4, wherein the interval comprises one of: a skin close to patient out interval; a patient out to case open interval; a case open to patient in interval; 

 a patient in to skin cut interval; and a wheels out to wheels in interval. 6. The computer-implemented method of Claim 4, wherein the method further comprises: determining a third metric result, the third metric result comprising at least one of: a metric result associated with temporal workflow; a metric result associated with scheduling; and a metric result associated with human resources. 7. The computer-implemented method of Claim 6, wherein determining the third metric result comprises determining one or more of: an Efficiency metric result; a Consistency metric result; an Adverse Events metric result; a Case Volume metric result; a First Case Turnovers metric result; a Delay metric result; an OR Traffic metric result; a Room Layout metric result; and a Modality Conversion metric result. 8. The computer-implemented method of Claim 6, wherein the third metric result comprises a composite score result, the composite score result determined based upon one or more of the following component results: an Efficiency metric result; a Consistency metric result; an Adverse Events metric result; a Case Volume metric result; a First Case Turnovers metric result; a Delay metric result; 

 an OR Traffic metric result; a Room Layout metric result; and a Modality Conversion metric result. 9. The computer-implemented method of Claim 8, wherein the composite score result is a weighted average of one or more of the component results. 10. The computer-implemented method of Claim 6, wherein the method further comprises: determining a duration of an action in the theater-wide data; and determining a fourth metric result based upon the duration of the action. 11 . The computer-implemented method of Claim 10, wherein the method further comprises: determining, from the theater-wide data, a number of personnel in the theater during at least a portion of the nonoperative period; and determining a fifth metric result based upon the determined number of personnel. 12. The computer-implemented method of Claim 11 , wherein the method further comprises: detecting an inefficiency, at least in part, by comparing: a cluster of historical metric results; and a group of metric results comprising two or more of: the first metric result; the second metric result; the third metric result; the fourth metric result; and the fifth metric result. 13. The computer-implemented method of Claim 12, wherein detecting an inefficiency, comprises: 

 determining a distance in a space having dimensions corresponding to the metric results of the group of metric results, the distance between: at least a portion of the cluster of historical metric results; and a point associated with the group of metric results. 14. The computer-implemented method of Claim 12, wherein detecting an inefficiency, comprises: determining that a point associated with the group of metric results exceeds a standard deviation from the cluster of historical metric results. 15. The computer-implemented method of one of Claims 1 -14, wherein determining the motion of the team member comprises: determining an optical flow of at least a portion of the theater-wide data; determining that the optical flow of the at least a portion of the theater-wide data corresponds to the team member; and determining a standardized motion associated with the optical flow. 16. The computer-implemented method of one of Claims 1 -14, wherein determining the motion of the team member comprises: applying a transformer-based attention model to at least a portion of the theaterwide data. 17. The computer-implemented method of one of Claims 1-14, wherein the method further comprises: distinguishing the nonoperative period in the theater-wide data from an operative period in the theater-wide data using a transformer configured to implement a gated recurrent unit method for detecting nonoperative periods in theater-wide data. 18. The computer-implemented method of one of Claims 1 -14, wherein detecting the team member comprises: 

 applying a transformer-based spatial model to at least a portion of the theaterwide data. 19. The computer-implemented method of one of Claims 4-14, wherein detecting the interval in the theater-wide data comprises: applying a transformer configured to implement a gated recurrent unit method to at least a portion of the theater-wide data. 20. The computer-implemented method of one of Claims 11 -14, wherein the method further comprises: determining the number of personnel in the theater during the at least the portion of the nonoperative period comprises applying a transformer based spatial model configured to process theater-wide data. 21 . A non-transitory computer-readable medium comprising instructions configured to cause at least one computer system to perform a method for assessing team member performance during a nonoperative period in a surgical theater, the method comprising: detecting a team member from theater-wide data acquired during the nonoperative period; determining a motion of the team member; and determining a first metric result based, at least in part, upon the motion of the team member. 22.
